channel.title: Science
channel.description: The very best in news and research across the sciences.

item.title: Wolves are reconquering Europe. Can people learn to live with them?
item.description: As populations rebound, attacks on livestock and humans are fueling debate over the limits of coexistence

item.title: Decoupling of global metabolic flux and proteome partitioning in bacteria | Science
item.description: Bacteria regulate homeostatic growth by adjusting proteome composition. In Escherichia coli, this coordination is mediated by guanosine tetraphosphate and pentaphosphate, collectively termed (p)ppGpp, which couple amino acid supply with ribsosome ...

item.title: Age and early life adversity shape heterogeneity of the epigenome across tissues in macaques | Science
item.description: Age and early life adversity (ELA) are key determinants of health, but whether they affect similar physiological mechanisms across tissues is unknown. We generated DNA methylation (DNAm) profiles across 14 tissues in 237 semi–free-ranging rhesus macaques ...

item.title: Maternal trans-vaccenic acid shapes neonatal T cell development and early-life immune imprinting | Science
item.description: How maternal nutrition influences neonatal immune development and imprinting through breastfeeding remains largely unclear. We report that maternal supplementation with trans-vaccenic acid (TVA), the predominant naturally occurring trans-fatty acid in ...

item.title: Lamprey 3D single-cell transcriptomics reveals ancestral and specialized features of the vertebrate brain | Science
item.description: The lamprey occupies a pivotal position for elucidating vertebrate brain evolution. Using spatial transcriptomics and single-nucleus RNA sequencing, we generated a three-dimensional molecular atlas of the lamprey brain, identifying 209 distinct cell ...

item.title: Brain-wide topographic coordination of rotating waves | Science
item.description: Patterns of brain activity moving in waves occur across brain regions and species, yet their spatial organization, anatomical basis, and brain-wide distribution remain unclear. Using cortex-wide imaging and electrophysiology in awake mice, we revealed a ...

item.title: CD4+ T cells impair tumor growth through IL-3 and TNF-dependent vascular damage | Science
item.description: Most cancer immunotherapy strategies are focused on direct tumor killing by immune cells, especially T lymphocytes. Clinical and conceptual limitations of these approaches create a need for additional strategies. We identified a tumor stroma–targeting ...

item.title: Managing the pitfalls of private money | Science
item.description: In recent months, we have learned that the relationship between the US federal government and American science is not as stable as many of us thought it to be. While many grants that were abruptly (and perhaps illegally) cut earlier this year have been restored, the relationship between science and the federal government that has prevailed since the 1950s has shifted. The decades ahead are unlikely to look like the ones of the recent past.

item.title: In Other Journals | Science
item.description: Editors’ selections from the current scientific literature

item.title: In Science Journals | Science
item.description: Highlights from the Science family of journals

item.title: Inheriting trauma, rethinking science | Science
item.description: HomeScienceVol. 392, No. 6804Inheriting trauma, rethinking scienceBack To Vol. 392, No. 6804 Full accessLetterPast as Prologue Share on Inheriting trauma, rethinking scienceRana DajaniAuthors Info & AffiliationsScience18 Jun 2026Vol 392, Issue 6804p. 1251DOI: 10.1126/science.aeh7938 PREVIOUS ARTICLEAI may raise the bar and thin the pipelinePreviousNEXT ARTICLEIn Science JournalsNext Notification…

item.title: AI may raise the bar and thin the pipeline | Science
item.description: In their Perspective “AI raises the productivity bar” (19 February, 10.1126/science.aef5239), L. Wu and B. Vasilescu (1) argue that artificial intelligence (AI) can increase productivity while rewarding workers who already possess strong evaluative and delegation skills. However, they do not address the most important implication of their model. If expertise in evaluation and delegation is now a stronger precondition for productivity than the ability to execute tasks oneself, firms and laboratories may respond not by shedding large numbers of incumbent workers but by quietly reducing entry-level opportunities (1). In that case, aggregate output may remain stable or even rise while the apprenticeship function of organizations weakens. To ensure longterm success, researchers and organizations should assess a broad range of variables that adopting AI could affect.

item.title: Preserve EEOC data collection | Science
item.description: HomeScienceVol. 392, No. 6804Preserve EEOC data collectionBack To Vol. 392, No. 6804 Full accessLetter Share on Preserve EEOC data collectionFidan Ana Kurtulus, Edo Navot, and Donald Tomaskovic-DeveyAuthors Info & AffiliationsScience18 Jun 2026Vol 392, Issue 6804p. 1250DOI: 10.1126/science.aei1236 PREVIOUS ARTICLEReanimating ELIZA, the world’s first chatbotPreviousNEXT ARTICLEAI may raise the bar…

item.title: Reanimating ELIZA, the world’s first chatbot | Science
item.description: A simple program created in the 1960s laid the groundwork for future conversational computers

item.title: Making the most of your time at work | Science
item.description: Thinking strategically from the start could help you do more good over the course of your career

item.title: Scientific computing in an AI world | Science
item.description: Scientific computing must integrate AI with simulation and focus on energy-efficient methods and systems

item.title: Tracking the roots of zoonoses | Science
item.description: Ancient pathogen DNA from animals elucidates the origin and evolution of infectious diseases

item.title: An HIV vaccine blueprint | Science
item.description: The infection of rhesus macaques with a chimeric virus uncovers a recipe for broadly neutralizing antibodies

item.title: A metastable solid solution transforms into complex nanostructures | Science
item.description: Instability in a high-entropy alloy drives structural changes that strengthen the alloy

item.title: Violet seed propulsion inspires robot design | Science
item.description: Comparative analysis of seed biomechanics reveals principles that can guide soft robot technology

item.title: Britain’s next research choice | Science
item.description: Ten years ago, the Brexit referendum began the United Kingdom’s departure from the European Union (EU), forcing British science to confront a serious question: What place should the UK occupy in European research? No longer qualified to be a full-fledged ...

item.title: A space telescope is falling to Earth. NASA is racing to rescue it
item.description: Vehicle will attempt a daring capture-and-boost mission to extend the life of the Swift observatory

item.title: Big Ebola outbreak puts spotlight on little-known virus
item.description: The Bundibugyo virus only emerged twice before. Now, scientists see a chance to get to know it better

item.title: Russia’s plan to drill superdeep holes in Arctic revives controversial theory of ‘endless oil’
item.description: Soviet-era theory touted by Putin’s former campaign manager claims oil deposits can form without organic matter

item.title: AI models have a troubling knack for discovering legal loopholes
item.description: AIs on their own found ways to exploit regulations and evade current safeguards

item.title: Metal-driven chemical reaction in deep sea may explain origin of life
item.description: Group offers new scenario for how cells came to rely on phosphate molecules to build DNA and store energy

item.title: El Niño has begun. It may become the strongest this century
item.description: After clearing a spring forecasting hurdle, scientists see growing odds of a powerful climate event that could disrupt weather worldwide

item.title: ‘Weird and capricious’: Experts struggle to understand new list of political jobs at science agencies
item.description: NIH programs and leadership are hit especially hard, while other science agencies are barely mentioned

item.title: Another red alert for American science | Science
item.description: Although research has bipartisan support in the US Congress, and trust in science is above 75% across the country, the Trump administration seems as determined as ever to mortally wound the nation’s scientific enterprise. After the scientific community ...

item.title: Induction of broadly neutralizing HIV antibodies by a two-step mechanism informs vaccine design | Science
item.description: A major obstacle confronting HIV-1 vaccine and cure research is the lack of an outbred animal model for rapid and consistent induction of broadly neutralizing antibodies (bNAbs). We designed an epitope-focused simian-human immunodeficiency virus (...
